title: GShard: Scaling Giant Models with Conditional Computation and Automatic Sharding
authors: Dmitry Lepikhin, HyoukJoong Lee, Yuanzhong Xu, Dehao Chen et al.
hook: GShard trains a 600-billion parameter model by assigning work only where needed.
promise: You will understand how GShard uses conditional computation and sharding to train enormous sparse models efficiently.
prerequisite_note: You only need a sense for how neural networks use lots of matrix math and benefit from splitting work.

glossary.plain: Only some parts of the model are activated for each input, saving effort.
A setup where many expert sub-models exist, but only a few are used per example.
Splitting a large model or data across multiple machines for parallel processing.
A compiler for linear algebra that helps optimize and distribute computations on accelerators.
Tensor Processing Unit, a specialized chip designed to accelerate machine learning tasks.

cards.title: How to Scale AI Giants
body: Discover how GShard trains huge models by splitting work and using only what is needed.

cards.title: Why do only some neurons work?
body: Imagine a team of experts, but only a few are called for each question. This is how GShard thinks about massive neural networks.
Instead of using every part of a huge model for every input, GShard activates only the most relevant experts. This saves time, memory, and energy.

cards.title: From dense models to sparse, expert-driven giants
body: Classical neural networks process every input through the entire model, which is costly at scale.
GShard introduces a way to split the model into many 'experts' and only use some for each input. This is conditional computation.
Automatic sharding then spreads these experts across many machines, letting the system train models far larger than before.
cards.caption: Conditional computation and sharding make massive models practical to train.

cards.title: Dense vs. Sparse: What changes?
body: Toggle to see how using all neurons compares to activating only a few experts.
cards.offNote: Every neuron runs for every input, making training slow and memory-hungry.
cards.onNote: Only a few experts run, saving resources and enabling much larger models.

cards.title: Conditional computation plus smart splitting
body: The core trick is to use a Mixture-of-Experts layer, where a router picks which experts to activate for each input.
GShard's annotation API lets researchers mark which parts to shard, and the XLA extension automatically splits the model across many TPUs.

cards.title: How many experts should you activate?
body: Slide to see the impact of activating more or fewer experts per input.
cards.low: Lowest computation, may miss important patterns.
cards.mid: Balanced: enough experts for quality, but still efficient.
cards.high: Maximum computation, best quality but expensive and slow.

cards.title: What does GShard need?
body: Pick the ingredients required to scale up a giant sparse model like GShard.

choices.label: Mixture-of-Experts layer
choices.note: Creates many sub-models (experts) to choose from.

choices.label: Conditional routing
choices.note: Selects which experts should handle each input.

choices.label: Sharding API
choices.note: Lets you specify how to split the model.

choices.label: XLA compiler extension
choices.note: Automates the process of distributing computation.

choices.label: TPU cluster
choices.note: Provides the hardware muscle for training at scale.

choices.label: Multilingual data
choices.note: Gives the model a rich, diverse training set to learn from.

cards.title: GShard's giant leap: What changed?
body: GShard trained a multilingual translation model with over 600 billion parameters, much larger than previous systems.
It achieved better translation quality across 100 languages and trained in just four days using 2048 TPUs.
cards.caption: GShard made it possible to train much larger models faster and with better results.

cards.title: Why GShard changed the game
body: GShard proved that huge models can be trained efficiently by combining conditional computation and automatic sharding.
This opened the door for even larger and more capable language models, changing how researchers think about scaling.

recap.label: Split
recap.note: Divide the model into many experts.

recap.label: Route
recap.note: Send each input to just a few relevant experts.

recap.label: Shard
recap.note: Distribute the model automatically across many machines.

recap.label: Scale
recap.note: Train models hundreds of times larger, efficiently.

cards.title: GShard: Scaling Giant Models with Conditional Computation and Automatic Sharding
body: Read the original paper to see how GShard made massive sparse models practical.
